messages.content: Could you help me check my manuscript to see if there's anything that can be polished?
Of course! I'd be happy to help you. Please provide me with your manuscript, and I'll review it to see if there are any areas that can be polished.

Table 4 includes a list of the samples’ trace element as well as the corresponding average PAAS content for comparison. The samples’ most enriched elements are Sr, Ba, and Th, all of which have average abundances above 1 mg/kg. The ratio of the trace element abundance is a useful indicator for quantitatively assessing the degree of element enrichment the samples from the three formations EF values fall between 0.38 and 2.67, indicating that their trace element contents are comparable to those of PAAS (Fig. 11). With EF averages of 1.69 and 1.50, respectively, the shale samples are significantly enriched in Sr and Ba. With EF averages that are all above 1, Cu, Rb, and Th are also relatively enriched. Ni and Co, on the other hand, have relatively low concentrations in the samples, with average EF values of 0.67 and 0.74, respectively. The samples’ EF values demonstrate stratigraphic differentiation.

Table 4 presents a comprehensive list of trace elements in the samples, along with their corresponding average content in the PAAS standard for comparison. Notably, the samples exhibit high enrichments in Sr, Ba, and Th, with average abundances exceeding 1 mg/kg. The ratio of trace element abundance serves as a valuable indicator to assess the extent of enrichment in the samples from the three formations. The EF values of these formations range from 0.38 to 2.67, indicating that their trace element contents are comparable to those of PAAS (refer to Fig. 11). Specifically, the shale samples show significant enrichments in Sr and Ba, with EF averages of 1.69 and 1.50, respectively. Additionally, Cu, Rb, and Th are also relatively enriched, with EF averages exceeding 1. Conversely, Ni and Co exhibit lower concentrations in the samples, with average EF values of 0.67 and 0.74, respectively. It is worth noting that the EF values of the samples demonstrate a clear stratigraphic differentiation.

Measured δ 13Ccarb values from the analyzed samples range from -4.9‰ to 2.5‰ (mean = -0.89‰). Values in the E1f2Sq1 range from 0.6‰ to 2.5‰ (mean = 1.8‰), the E1f2Sq2 range from -0.5‰ to 1.4‰ (mean = 0.52‰), while values from E1f2Sq3 display a wide range, from -4.9‰ to 1.0‰ (mean = -2.05‰). Measured δ 18Ocarb values from the analyzed samples range from -9.8‰ to -2.8‰ (mean = -5.89‰). Values in the E1f2Sq1 range from -9.8‰ to -7.7‰ (mean = -8.67‰), the E1f2Sq2 range from -9.0‰ to -5.3‰ (mean = -7.08‰), while values from E1f2Sq3 display a wide range, from -7.7‰ to -2.8‰ (mean = -4.79‰). Thus, δ13Ccarb values of the Funing Formation generally increase from bottom to top, but δ 18Ocarb values shows completely opposite characteristics (Table 5).

The measured δ13Ccarb values obtained from the analyzed samples exhibit a range of -4.9‰ to 2.5‰, with a mean value of -0.89‰. In the E1f2Sq1 formation, the values span from 0.6‰ to 2.5‰, with a mean of 1.8‰. The E1f2Sq2 formation values range from -0.5‰ to 1.4‰, with a mean of 0.52‰. On the other hand, the E1f2Sq3 formation displays a broader range, with values ranging from -4.9‰ to 1.0‰ and a mean of -2.05‰. As for the measured δ18Ocarb values, they range from -9.8‰ to -2.8‰, with a mean value of -5.89‰. In the E1f2Sq1 formation, the values range from -9.8‰ to -7.7‰, with a mean value of -8.67‰. Similarly, the E1f2Sq2 formation exhibits values ranging from -9.0‰ to -5.3‰, and a mean of -7.08‰. Meanwhile, the E1f2Sq3 formation showcases a wider range, varying from -7.7‰ to -2.8‰, with a mean value of -4.79‰. Consequently, the δ13Ccarb values generally increase from the bottom to the top of the Funing Formation. In contrast, the δ18Ocarb values display completely opposite characteristics (as summarized in Table 5).

The restoration of the paleoenvironment is greatly aided by paleosalinity (Hou et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022). Sr is more mobile in water and has a higher solubility product from its sulfate compounds than Ba  (Liang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022). Ba first precipitates as the water's salinity rises gradually, and the Sr stays in the water until the salinity reaches a certain level. As a result, there is a positive correlation between the ratio of Sr/Ba in sediments and paleosalinity (Meng et al., 2012; Tao et al., 2017), with ratios of Sr/Ba 1.0 reflecting fresh and ratios >1 reflecting salty water, respectively. Shale samples from the E1f2Sq1, E1f2Sq2, and E1f2Sq3 have average Sr/Ba ratios of 0.47, 0.91, and 0.74, respectively, showing how the paleo-salinity changed from semi-sality, sality, to fresh (Fig. 12a).

The assessment of paleoenvironmental conditions greatly benefits from the use of paleosalinity data (Hou et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022). Sr exhibits higher mobility in water and has a higher solubility product compared to Ba in its sulfate compounds (Liang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022). Consequently, Ba precipitates as the water's salinity gradually increases, while Sr remains in the water until the salinity reaches a certain threshold. This leads to a positive correlation between the Sr/Ba ratio in sediments and paleosalinity (Meng et al., 2012; Tao et al., 2017), where Sr/Ba ratios of 1.0 and >1.0 indicate fresh and salty water conditions, respectively. Examining the shale samples from the E1f2Sq1, E1f2Sq2, and E1f2Sq3 formations, their average Sr/Ba ratios are 0.47, 0.91, and 0.74, respectively, which indicates a transition from semi-saline to saline, and finally to fresh water conditions in the paleo-environment (refer to Fig. 12a).

Sedimentary environments are controlled by palaeoclimates, which have a direct impact on many geological processes. Changes in the ratio and trace element distribution can be used to detect paleoclimate change (Vosoughi Moradi et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2017). Climates with low Sr content are wetter, while those with higher Sr content are drier. The sensitivity of Sr content to climate change. Therefore, Sr/Cu is a popular geochemical indicator for determining past climates, with values between 1.3-5 indicating warm and moist climates and values above 5 indicating hot and arid climates. (Bolarinwa et al., 2022; FU et al., 2018). Shale samples from the E1f2Sq1, E1f2Sq2, and E1f2Sq3 have average Sr/Cu values of 11.7, 20.9, and 31.0, respectively (Table 5), revealing the paleoclimatic transition from warm and humid to hot and arid (Fig. 12b).

Sedimentary environments are directly influenced by paleoclimates, which play a vital role in various geological processes. Detecting changes in the ratio and distribution of trace elements can help identify shifts in paleoclimate conditions (Vosoughi Moradi et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2017). Wetter climates are generally associated with lower Sr content, while drier climates exhibit higher Sr content. Due to Sr's sensitivity to climate change, the Sr/Cu ratio has become a widely used geochemical indicator for past climate reconstructions. Sr/Cu values ranging from 1.3 to 5 indicate warm and moist climates, while values above 5 suggest hot and arid climates (Bolarinwa et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2018). Analyzing the shale samples from the E1f2Sq1, E1f2Sq2, and E1f2Sq3 formations, their average Sr/Cu values are 11.7, 20.9, and 31.0, respectively (as shown in Table 5). These values indicate a paleoclimatic transition from warm and humid conditions to hot and arid conditions (as depicted in Fig. 12b).

Because redox condition controls how enrichments of certain elements because these elements are sensitive to particular redox environments, Indicators of redox state can be found in these substances and their indices. (Deng et al., 2019; Tribovillard et al., 2006).
Oxidative (V/ (V + Ni)) and anaerobic (V/ (V + Ni)) conditions are commonly used to assess the redox state of a given system. (Jones and Manning, 1994). The V/ (V + Ni) ratios of the samples in the study area range from 0.69 to 0.77, on average 0.73, which denotes anoxic water conditions. Ni/Co ratios of 0.5 and >0.5, which indicate oxic and anoxic conditions, respectively, are another helpful indicator for determining redox conditions (Rimmer, 2004). Shale samples from the study area's of Ni/Co indicates anoxic water conditions, which is the same as the result of V/(V + Ni) ratios (Fig. 12c).

You are correct in highlighting the significance of redox conditions in controlling element enrichments, as certain elements are highly sensitive to specific redox environments (Deng et al., 2019; Tribovillard et al., 2006). Assessing the redox state of a system often involves examining indicators found in substances and their associated indices. One commonly used method is the evaluation of oxidative (V/(V + Ni)) and anaerobic (V/(V + Ni)) conditions (Jones and Manning, 1994). In the study area, the V/(V + Ni) ratios of the samples range from 0.69 to 0.77, with an average of 0.73, suggesting anoxic water conditions. Another useful indicator for determining redox conditions is the Ni/Co ratio. Values below 0.5 indicate oxic conditions, while values above 0.5 suggest anoxic conditions (Rimmer, 2004). In the shale samples from the study area, the Ni/Co ratios indicate anoxic water conditions, aligning with the results from the V/(V + Ni) ratios (as shown in Fig. 12c).

Because redox condition controls how enrichments of certain elements because these elements are sensitive to particular redox environments, Indicators of redox state can be found in these substances and their indices. (Deng et al., 2019; Tribovillard et al., 2006).

You are correct in emphasizing the influence of redox conditions on the enrichment of certain elements, as these elements exhibit sensitivity to specific redox environments. The indicators of redox state can indeed be identified in substances and their associated indices (Deng et al., 2019; Tribovillard et al., 2006).

Because redox condition controls how enrichments of certain elements because these elements are sensitive to particular redox environments, Indicators of redox state can be found in these substances and their indices.

Yes, you are absolutely correct. The redox condition plays a critical role in the enrichment of specific elements as these elements are highly sensitive to particular redox environments. Therefore, indicators of redox state can be identified within these substances and their associated indices. Understanding the redox conditions is crucial for deciphering the geological processes and environmental conditions that influenced the formation of the substances.

According to previous research results, the carbon and oxygen stable isotopes of the shale carbonate rocks in E1f2 are distributed in the second and third quadrants, and they are mainly distributed in or near the closed lake basins such as Great Salt Lake and Turkala (Fig. 12d)(Liu et al., 2001; Talbot, 1990), and there is an obvious correlation between δ13C and δ18O, indicating that the overall sealing of the lake basin is strong , and it is less affected by external water bodies.

Previous research findings suggest that the carbon and oxygen stable isotopes of the shale carbonate rocks in the E1f2 formation display a distribution within the second and third quadrants. These isotopes are primarily found in or near closed lake basins, such as the Great Salt Lake and Turkala (as shown in Fig. 12d) (Liu et al., 2001; Talbot, 1990). Additionally, there is a noticeable correlation between the δ13C and δ18O values, indicating a strong overall sealing of the lake basin and a reduced influence from external water bodies.

Based on all the above analysis, the lacustrine shale sedimentary setting in the E1f2 is straightforward to ascertain. Paleoenvironmental conditions include: humid to arid climate, anoxic salt-fresh closed water. The salty of the shale was generally fresh water, but there were also exceptions, such as a transgression during the deposition of Funing Formation. Based on the longitudinal variation of geochemical parameters (Fig. 13), the sedimentary environment of the Funing Formation can be generally divided into three depositional stages: 
1) E1f2Sq1: humid climate, fresh and anoxic closed lake; 2) E1f2Sq2: Arid climate, salty and anoxic open lake; 3) E1f2Sq3: humid climate, fresh and anoxic closed lake.

Based on the comprehensive analysis conducted, the lacustrine shale sedimentary setting in the E1f2 formation can be confidently identified. The paleoenvironmental conditions during deposition indicate a transition from a humid to arid climate, characterized by anoxic, salt-fresh closed water conditions. Although the shale samples generally exhibit characteristics of fresh water, there are exceptions, such as a transgression during the deposition of the Funing Formation. Considering the longitudinal variation of geochemical parameters (as depicted in Fig. 13), the sedimentary environment of the Funing Formation can be broadly divided into three distinct depositional stages:
1) E1f2Sq1: Deposition occurred in a humid climate, within a fresh and anoxic closed lake setting.
2) E1f2Sq2: Deposition occurred in an arid climate, within a salty and anoxic open lake setting.
3) E1f2Sq3: Deposition occurred in a humid climate, within a fresh and anoxic closed lake setting.

The production and preservation of OM are typically required for the complex process of OM enrichment (Killops et al., 2022). The primary factor affecting organism variety, growth, and decomposition is the paleoenvironment, which includes paleoclimate, redox condition, and paleosalinity. As a result, it regulates the production and preservation of OM (Erdenetsogt et al., 2022; Rohais et al., 2019).

You are absolutely correct. The production and preservation of organic matter (OM) are crucial for the complex process of OM enrichment (Killops et al., 2022). The paleoenvironment, which encompasses factors such as paleoclimate, redox conditions, and paleosalinity, plays a significant role in influencing the variety, growth, and decomposition of organisms. These factors ultimately regulate the production and preservation of OM (Erdenetsogt et al., 2022; Rohais et al., 2019). Understanding the interplay between these paleoenvironmental factors is crucial for comprehending the formation and distribution of organic matter within sedimentary settings.

The accumulations of organisms and their preservations are both greatly influenced by the salinity of the environment, and relatively low water column salinity is advantageous for OM preservation (Jin et al., 2022), which is corroborated by the fact that Sr/Ba levels tend to be inversely proportional to TOC levels. (Fig. 14a). The transgression caused the water columns' salinity to rise from the E1f2Sq3 to the E1f2Sq2, which is probably a significant contributor to the low TOC in the E1f2Sq2 and E1f2Sq1. Warm and humid conditions are more better to organism growths than hot and arid ones, which means they can encourage the production of OM (Rahman et al., 2020). The TOC contents and Sr/Cu exhibit a clear inverse relationship (Fig. 14b), demonstrating that OM enrichment is favored by warm, humid climates. The TOC contents decrease as the sedimentary becomes hotter and more arid.

Indeed, the salinity of the environment has a significant impact on the accumulation and preservation of organisms, and lower water column salinity generally promotes organic matter (OM) preservation (Jin et al., 2022). This is supported by the inverse relationship between Sr/Ba levels and total organic carbon (TOC) levels (as shown in Fig. 14a). The transgression event resulted in an increase in water column salinity from E1f2Sq3 to E1f2Sq2, which likely contributed to the lower TOC content in E1f2Sq2 and E1f2Sq1. Additionally, warm and humid conditions are more favorable for organism growth and
